The Golden Horsemen

# The Golden Horsemen

### The Scythians and Sakas, from the Eurasian steppe to India.

[](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

![Ravinder Reddy's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2Tvm!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F77da9531-28fb-41f8-b757-599e5b73f4c6_1202x1204.png)

[Ravinder Reddy](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

Jun 10, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bUrT!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6cc339fb-abce-4c34-8545-4c742bc766c4_2590x1880.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bUrT!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6cc339fb-abce-4c34-8545-4c742bc766c4_2590x1880.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sometime around 530 BCE, a Persian army crossed a river in Central Asia, the Araxes (probably the Syr Darya or the Amu Darya), to conquer a horse nomad people called the Massagetae. The Persians were led by Cyrus the Great, founder of the largest empire the world had yet seen, a man whose rule extended over Babylon, Lydia, and Media. The nomads were led by a widow named Tomyris.

Cyrus tricked the first wave of Massagetae nomads with a banquet of wine, a drink these nomads barely knew, and slaughtered them while they lay drunk. Among the captured was the queen’s son, who killed himself in shame. Tomyris sent Cyrus a message: “Your wine is a poison that makes men mad. You thirst for blood, Cyrus, and I swear by the Sun, the great god of my people, I will give you your fill of it.” Then she met him on the battlefield and destroyed his army. She found Cyrus’s corpse, cut off his head, and shoved it into a skin filled with human blood. She then told the Shahanshah, “Now, drink your fill.”

We’re entering a world of horse archers, golden art, and human sacrifice. Women ruled, men resisted; the Greeks called them Scythians, the Persians Saka, and the Indians Shaka, eventually absorbing them. They left no written records, so our knowledge comes from enemies, contemporaries, graves, and, more recently, genetics.

## I. Origins: where the Scythians came from

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Scythians come from the Eurasian steppe, which stretches from the plains of Hungary across the Black Sea, the Volga, and the Caspian, through modern-day Kazakhstan up into the Altai, the largest temperate grassland on Earth, an ocean of grass with no trees to stop the wind or the horse.

In the classical imagination, they appear suddenly in the eighth and seventh centuries BCE, pouring out of the east, pushing aside an earlier people called the Cimmerians, raiding down into Assyria and Anatolia and the fringes of the Greek world. For a generation in the seventh century BCE they were a terror across the Near East, and they were considered to be among the wolves that helped take down the dying Assyrian Empire. Much of what we know about their origin story comes from Herodotus, the famous Greek historian who wrote about them around 430 BCE.

The Scythians’ own legend, as he recorded it, runs like this. The first man was called Targitaus, son of Zeus and a daughter of the river Borysthenes (the Dnipro). Targitaus had three sons: Lipoxais, Arpoxais, and Kolaxais. While they ruled, four golden objects fell from the sky:

- a plough and a yoke(together)

- a battle-axe

- a cup

When the two elder brothers approached the gold, it burst into flame. Only when the youngest, Kolaxais, came near did the fire cool, and so he gathered up the gold and became king. The scholar Bruce Lincoln (University of Chicago) reads the origin story like this:

- the plough and yoke are the tools of the farmer

- the axe is the weapon of the warrior

- the cup is the vessel of the priest

It is the same three-way division of society, into producer, fighter, and priest, that ran through every early Indo-European society from Rome to India.

There is a second origin tale, the one the Greeks who lived around the Black Sea preferred, likely because it put their own hero at the base of the myth. Heracles, wandering the empty north, fell asleep and woke to find his horses stolen. He found them in the keeping of a creature that was a beautiful woman above the waist and a serpent below. She would return the horses only if he lay with her. He complied, and she bore three sons. The youngest, Scythes, was the only one strong enough to string his father’s great bow and buckle his belt, and so the land and the line were his.

The Scythian genetics tell a very interesting story. For now, hold this fact. The Scythians were not a single tribe with a single homeland. “Scythian” is a label, like “Viking” or “Celt,” for a whole family of related horse peoples spread across thousands of miles, speaking Iranian dialects, sharing a way of life, a style of art, and sometimes a similar way of death. The Greeks met the western ones on the Black Sea. The Persians met the eastern ones, whom they called Saka.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ELDz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F00a7db1c-b170-4983-8986-321896a28837_1280x1380.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ELDz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F00a7db1c-b170-4983-8986-321896a28837_1280x1380.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Persians knew them well, because they kept trying to conquer the Scythians and kept failing. Darius the Great carved a great inscription into a cliff at Behistun around 519 BCE listing the peoples he ruled, and among them are the Saka (likely the first appearance of the name in the historical record). Persian sources sort them into three kinds:

- the **Saka Haumavarga**, the “haoma-drinking” Saka, named for the
  sacred plant of the Iranian priests

- the **Saka Tigraxauda**, the “pointed-cap” Saka, instantly known by
  their tall conical hats

- the **Saka Paradraya**, the Saka “beyond the sea,” the western
  Scythians of the Black Sea steppe

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TZfo!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F38acd036-9eb4-4089-a588-9905b96fd70d_874x1850.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TZfo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F38acd036-9eb4-4089-a588-9905b96fd70d_874x1850.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Cataphract-style parade armor of a Saka royal, popularly known as the “Golden Warrior” or “Golden Man,” from the Issyk kurgan near Almaty, Kazakhstan, c. 4th–3rd century BCE.**

Even this seemingly neat threefold division isn’t really doing justice to the reality, because the modern interpretation keeps getting it wrong (you can see this all over X when Scythians are discussed). “Saka” is not a nation name. It was the Persians’ catch-all term for the horse nomads beyond their frontier, the way every settled empire slaps a single word on the blur of tribes at its edge. There was no united Saka confederation and no single Saka people in the genetic sense. The Scythian/Saka label covered dozens of tribes and clans, who were sometimes at war with one another, spread over thousands of miles, related in language and way of life but ruled from no central city, and they most definitely did not spring from a single stock. When the genomes finally came in and got analysed, as we will see, they confirmed exactly this.

Darius learned the same lesson Cyrus had. Around 513 BCE he led a vast army across the Danube into Scythian territory to crush them once and for all. The Scythians simply refused to fight a pitched battle. They retreated, destroyed their own water supply, burned the grass behind them, and let the Persian army wander and starve across an empty land until Darius gave up and went home. It is one of the oldest recorded examples of a strategy that would later humble Napoleon and Hitler in the same part of the world.

You cannot conquer people who have nothing to defend and nowhere you can pin them down.

So where did they actually come from? For a long time, scholars argued between a western origin, on the Black Sea steppe, and an eastern one, deep in Siberia. The newest evidence, including a single richly buried Iron Age warrior from Chinge-Tey in the Tuva region of southern Siberia, leans east. That warrior is older than the famous frozen tombs of the Altai and far older than Herodotus’s Black Sea Scythians, and he is genetically more East Eurasian than any of them. The whole Scythian package as we now know it, the animal art, the kurgan burials, the mounted-archer way of life, seems to have taken shape in the eastern steppe and rolled west, not the other way round as some scholars suggested earlier. The riders Herodotus met on the edge of the Greek world were the far western tip of something that had begun thousands of miles away.

And they came to India, eventually, not as conquerors but as refugees. Far to the east, on the frontier of China, a confederation called the Xiongnu pressed on a people called the Yuezhi. The Yuezhi, pushed off their land, crashed into the Saka of the Ili valley and drove them south. The Saka, in turn, spilled through the mountain passes into Bactria and down into the Indus. It was a chain reaction where the last domino fell in India. The Greeks and Romans even renamed the lower Indus after them: Indo-Scythia, the land of the Saka. They did not choose India. They were pushed into it. And then, as we will see, India did to them what India does.

## II. Hearth, sword, and smoke: Scythian religion

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

From what we know so far about the Scythians, it’s fair to assume that they built no temples or major religious monuments. To understand them, you have to understand a religion with no temples and few idols; it was a faith carried on horseback and written in fire, blood, and hemp smoke.

Herodotus depicts the whole Scythian pantheon in a single paragraph, naming each Scythian god by its nearest Greek equivalent. Edward Minns, whose great 1913 book *Scythians and Greeks* is still a foundation of the field, lays it out:

> The following deities were common to all, Tabiti, Hestia, who was the
> principal object of their veneration, next to her Papaeus, Zeus, with
> Apia, Ge, husband and wife, after them Goetosyrus, Apollo, Argimpasa,
> Aphrodite Urania, and Ares. Thamimasadas, Poseidon, was peculiar to
> the Royal Scyths. They raised no statues, altars or temples to their
> gods, save to Ares alone.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9x2b!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F04ef8848-7032-4f21-ba58-a67ee081e302_863x1822.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9x2b!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F04ef8848-7032-4f21-ba58-a67ee081e302_863x1822.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Karagodeuashkh headdress plate, a gold ceremonial diadem from a Scythian royal kurgan in the Kuban region, 4th century BCE.** Top to bottom, the plate is arranged in four registers of Scythian ritual hierarchy: at the apex, a small standing goddess appears in a niche, the divine presence presiding over everything below. Beneath her is the central scene: an enthroned female deity flanked by two horses, the classic Scythian “Great Goddess with horses” composition tied to Tabiti and royal investiture. Below that, a narrow frieze of fish or sea creatures marks the boundary between divine and human realms. At the bottom, a row of five human attendants performs what looks like a libation or offering rite, the mortal worshippers completing the chain from goddess to king to people

When you look at who sits at the top, it’s not the typical sky father we see in other Indo-European societies, not the war-god, but Tabiti, the goddess of the hearth and the sacred fire. The Russian scholar Bessonova built her whole reconstruction of Scythian religion around this hierarchy. According to her, the pantheon is hearth-centered and deeply Iranian, a seven-god system arranged in tiers that mirrors the religion of the Persians across the mountains, with their supreme god and his six holy immortals. To swear by the royal hearth of the Scythians was the most binding oath a man could take, exactly as the Persians swore by the royal fire. These were Iranian people, cousins of the Medes and Persians, and their gods were Iranian gods wearing Greek names that Herodotus pinned on them so his readers could follow along.

*Note: “Tabiti” is the Greek spelling of a Scythian word that linguists reconstruct as Tapatī, “the Burning One,” from an old Indo-Iranian root for heat and flame. It is the same root that gives Sanskrit tapati, “it burns,” and tapas, the Vedic word for the inner heat of ritual and ascetic power, and even the name of the Vedic figure Tapati. The fact that the words are cousins does not prove the Scythians thought about her exactly as the Vedic tradition thought about tapas.*

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ssU5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F48918277-b492-4a4f-b7ee-b13b4a1a7f8e_1902x1842.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ssU5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F48918277-b492-4a4f-b7ee-b13b4a1a7f8e_1902x1842.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Scythian plaque showing a man before a deity, possibly the goddess Tabiti, 7th–3rd century BCE.** The scene appears to show a male figure facing a divine or semi-divine female figure, perhaps in a ritual or offering context. It is difficult to make out the details, but both figures seem to hold cups in their hands, suggesting a libation or ceremonial exchange. The object on the right may be an altar, possibly with a sacred fire, which would fit a ritual scene associated with worship, oath-taking, or royal-sacred investiture.

Then there is the exception, the one god who got an altar. The Scythian war god, called Ares, was worshipped not as a statue but as a sword. In every district, the Scythians piled up an enormous mound of brushwood, three furlongs broad and long, and on top of it they planted an ancient iron blade. Herodotus describes the rite as follows (translation by A. D. Godley):

> On this pile there is set for each people an ancient scimitar of iron,
> which is their image of Ares; to this scimitar they bring yearly
> sacrifice of sheep and goats and horses, offering to these symbols
> even more than they do to the other gods. Of all their enemies that
> they take alive, they sacrifice one man in every hundred, not
> according to their fashion of sacrificing sheep and goats, but
> differently. They pour wine on the men’s heads and cut their throats
> over a vessel; then they carry the blood up on to the pile of sticks
> and pour it on the scimitar.

A god you could hold in your hand, and feed with the blood of your enemies. The sword in the sacred mound is one of the most haunting images the ancient Scythian world has left us, and, as we will see at the very end of this piece, it did not die with the Scythians. It rode west into a legend we already know.

The Scythians also had a class of diviners that fascinated and unsettled the Greeks: the Enarei (priestesses and soothsayers), who were born male but lived as women, dressed as women, and were said to have received their second sight, and their changed nature, as a gift or a curse from the goddess of love. Herodotus put it down to a “feminine sickness” sent by Aphrodite in punishment for some ancestor’s looting of her temple. The Enarei look like one of the few documented institutions of a recognized third gender holding religious authority anywhere in the ancient world. They told the future by unwinding bundles of willow rods through their fingers, a method of divination that, remarkably, still survived among their descendants in the Caucasus thousands of years later.

And then there was death, which the Scythians did with grandeur. When a king died, his body was embalmed, drawn through his territory on a wagon so every subject and tribe could mourn him, and finally laid in a great chamber under the earth. Herodotus again tells what went into the grave with the king:

> They strangle and lay in the vacant room within the tomb one of the
> dead man’s concubines, and his cupbearer, his cook, his groom, and his
> messenger and horses, and cups of gold. When they have done this they
> make a great mound, vying with each other to make it as great as
> possible. After the lapse of a year they take fifty of the king’s best
> attendants, and fifty of the finest horses, slay them, and stuff them
> with chaff. One of the fifty strangled youths is then put astride of
> each horse. So these horses are set in a circle about the tomb.

*Note: the frozen Pazyryk tombs found in the Altai vindicated Herodotus’s account centuries later.*

Let’s talk about one more Scythian rite, which is talked about quite often in popular discourse around Scythians and… cannabis. Herodotus reports that after a funeral, the Scythians purified themselves not with water but with smoke. They set up a small tent, heated stones inside it, threw hemp seed on the stones, and sat in the rising vapor. Godley’s translation says this:

> The Scythians then take the seed of this hemp and, creeping under the
> rugs, they throw it on the red-hot stones; and, being so thrown, it
> smoulders and sends forth so much steam that no Greek vapour-bath
> could surpass it. The Scythians howl in joy for the vapour-bath.

For many centuries this was just a traveller’s tale, and academia did not believe most of what Herodotus wrote about them (however, he was vindicated again). In 1948, the Soviet archaeologist Sergei Rudenko opened the frozen tombs of Pazyryk in the Altai and found, beside the bodies, exactly what Herodotus had described: little six-legged pole frames for a tent, bronze censers, and inside them the charred seeds of *Cannabis sativa*. Rudenko quotes an ancient lexicon that the Scythian “smoking” was so strong it brought a sweat out of anyone who tried it, and he notes dryly that he found the apparatus buried with both a man and a woman, “since both men and women probably smoked.” Twenty-four centuries after Herodotus, the seeds were still in the burner.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sjOJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3fda27cb-3931-4a87-bef4-7f7f6ea1631d_539x477.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sjOJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3fda27cb-3931-4a87-bef4-7f7f6ea1631d_539x477.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Photograph of the actual carbonized Cannabis sativa seeds recovered from the burial chamber bronze vessel.

## III. The golden beasts: Scythian art

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If the Scythians had given the world nothing but their art, they would still be unforgettable. Academia refers to their art style as the Animal Style.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jLMt!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fda86ea5e-90a3-4a15-8563-cd951021ffce_1200x1130.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jLMt!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fda86ea5e-90a3-4a15-8563-cd951021ffce_1200x1130.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Reproduction of a Scythian plaque with animal combat, 19th century, after a 5th-century BCE original.

Picture a stag with its legs folded beneath it and its antlers sweeping back along its spine in a single rhythmic line. A panther curled into a perfect circle, biting its own tail. A griffin sinking its beak into the haunch of a horse, an eagle tearing a hare, a tangle of predators and prey worked in gold and bronze on every object a nomad owned: belt plaques, sword scabbards, horse harness, drinking cups, the gorgeous fittings of a saddle. The Scythians decorated their world with animals locked in the moment before the kill. Rudenko, who knew this art better than almost anyone, was careful about what it meant. The obvious guess is that they were sacred tribal animals. He rejected it:

> Neither the Greek historians nor the Chinese chroniclers make any
> reference to an animal cult among the Scyths. In sets of decoration
> from one outfit, that is, all worn by one horse, several different
> animals may be represented. This lends weight to our inference about
> the impossibility of the animals having a totemic significance.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KQ16!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8f772166-bff9-434a-a2f5-0ee4dc27d14c_1200x1076.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!KQ16!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8f772166-bff9-434a-a2f5-0ee4dc27d14c_1200x1076.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Reproduction of a Scythian plaque with animal combat, 19th century, after a 4th–3rd century BCE original.

It was not religion in the narrow sense. It was a way of seeing life, and the art represented that. The art and artifacts found so far show, in their way, a life lived among herds and hunters on an open plain, where everything was either predator or prey.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!a-qH!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe5bc57a1-edbd-4026-b44d-ffb47d72927a_960x1200.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!a-qH!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe5bc57a1-edbd-4026-b44d-ffb47d72927a_960x1200.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Belt buckle with paired felines attacking ibexes, Xiongnu, ca. 3rd–2nd century BCE.

If the beasts were not totems (spirit beings or sacred objects), some scholars suspect they were a map of the universe. When you closely study their art, you notice the same arrangement again and again: birds of prey above, hooved grazers in the middle, fanged predators sinking into them from below. Some authors and Scythian experts have suggested that the art encodes their world into three tiers:

- a sky of raptors

- a middle earth of stags and horses

- an underworld of monsters, with the predator forever falling on the
  prey at the seam between the worlds

Please do note that this is just an interpretation, and a very speculative one, since the Scythians left no literature for us to reference.

But I must say it does rhyme with something we have already discussed above. The four golden objects that fell from the sky in the Targitaus myth, the plough and yoke, the axe, and the cup, split society into three. The animal art may split the cosmos into three the same way. The habit of seeing the world in threes runs deep in Iranian-speaking peoples, as it does across the wider Indo-European family.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_FZI!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6b526a09-5a43-4e66-a2b6-0ff063abe3bf_1294x1216.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_FZI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6b526a09-5a43-4e66-a2b6-0ff063abe3bf_1294x1216.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Pazyryk carpet, one of the oldest surviving knotted-pile carpets in the world, c. 5th–4th century BCE.** Often described as Persian, Armenian, Achaemenid, or steppe-Iranian in style, its exact place of manufacture is still debated. The carpet was discovered in 1949 in a frozen Scythian/Pazyryk noble burial in the Pazyryk Valley of the Altai Mountains, and is now housed in the State Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, Russia. Its borders show rows of deer and horsemen, while the central field preserves a finely woven geometric design, making it one of the great masterpieces of ancient textile art.

The frozen tombs of Pazyryk were a significant discovery. The Siberian ice had preserved them like no other site could. Among the things the Russians uncovered were felt wall-hangings, one of them four and a half meters by six and a half, showing a goddess (likely Tabiti) enthroned with a rider before her, one of the few images of a Scythian deity we possess. There was the oldest knotted-pile carpet on Earth, worked at three thousand six hundred knots to the square decimeter, well over a million knots in all, bordered with rows of grazing stags and mounted men. Then there were the bodies themselves, embalmed with a surgical care that Rudenko describes: the organs removed, the cavity filled and sewn shut with horsehair thread, the skull packed with herbs.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6vqk!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F51965fc2-e79b-4d5e-8dc9-19186253ed5b_1916x732.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6vqk!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F51965fc2-e79b-4d5e-8dc9-19186253ed5b_1916x732.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CMMo!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc36a4826-f1a9-4ec3-a0d2-f3f4834b07fe_330x410.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CMMo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc36a4826-f1a9-4ec3-a0d2-f3f4834b07fe_330x410.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Pazyryk Barrow 2 chieftain was about 60, embalmed by skull trepanation and organ extraction, the cavities packed with grass and preservative the same way Egyptian morticians worked.

And there were the tattoos. On the body of a chieftain, Rudenko found skin covered from shoulder to wrist in the Animal Style: a horned beast over the heart, a donkey, a winged creature, a mountain ram, a griffin, all inked into him. He thought they “most probably signified noble birth or was a mark of manhood, or perhaps both.” In the Pazyryk burials we find the Animal Style not on a belt buckle but on a human arm. This clearly indicates that the Scythians considered animal art part of their expression, not mere body decoration.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GJy0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F74ee6453-3a89-4f9d-92df-906f0e80dca2_1600x1368.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GJy0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F74ee6453-3a89-4f9d-92df-906f0e80dca2_1600x1368.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Belt plaque from the Siberian Collection of Peter the Great, probably from the Ingala Valley, c. 300 BCE.** Sent to Peter the Great in 1716 by M. P. Gagarin, governor of Siberia in Tobolsk, this plaque belongs to the famous group of early steppe gold objects collected from Siberian burial sites

Then there is the gold. The Scythian kings were buried with a great deal of gold and wealth, and much of the finest work was made for them by Greek craftsmen along the Black Sea coast, who, as the scholar Artamonov put it, “seem to have been very willing to work for Scythian patrons,” being “perhaps too nervous of their behaviour, when angered, not to do so.” As you’ll see later, this Scytho-Greek connection lasts for a long time, and we see expressions of it in ancient India and Gandhara. My research on this topic indicates that, of all their contemporaries, the Greeks always had a very deep and intricate understanding of Scythian art and subjects. One such example is the great silver-gilt vase found at Chertomlyk. Here we see a very clear example of Greek depictions of Scythians breaking wild horses, dressed in their traditional clothing, typically long-haired, the older men bearded. On a gold comb from Solokha, three Scythian warriors fight to the death in exquisite miniature. Across the steppe, from the Kelermes panther curled on a shield to the later golden stags and pectorals dug from the kurgans, the same vision repeats.

Much of what we know about the Scythians came to light by accident and greed. At the end of the seventeenth century, Russian treasure hunters pushing east into Siberia, the bugrovshchiki, or mound-robbers, began breaking into the frozen kurgans of the Altai and pulling out the gold. So much of it flowed back west that in 1715 the governor of Siberia, Prince Gagarin, started shipping it to the Tsar. Peter the Great understood what it was, and in 1718 decreed that every ancient and unusual object dug from the ground be bought for the treasury and sent to St Petersburg, rather than melted down. The roughly 250 gold pieces that survived became the Siberian Collection of Peter the Great, the first archaeological collection in Russian history.

## IV. The genetics

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For most of history the Scythians were a riddle made of secondhand stories. In the last decade, ancient DNA has begun to answer this riddle.

In the Bronze Age, long before the Scythians, the eastern steppe was held by herding peoples we call Sintashta and Andronovo, descendants of the great westward and eastward expansion of the Yamnaya. They were overwhelmingly carriers of one male lineage, the Y-chromosome haplogroup R1a, and specifically its Asian branch, R1a-Z93. That lineage is the genetic thread that ties the Bronze Age steppe to the later Scythians.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2s2N!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6672a259-5708-4dc4-86d5-cdefd98df8b6_1073x1173.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2s2N!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6672a259-5708-4dc4-86d5-cdefd98df8b6_1073x1173.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Scythian elite were fiercely patrilineal. The pie charts track their Y-chromosome and maternal lineages; the family trees show that at some sites every buried man shared a single paternal line. Andreeva et al., Science Advances 2025.

Here comes the surprise. When the geneticist Gnecchi-Ruscone and colleagues sequenced one hundred and eleven individuals from the Kazakh steppe spanning fifteen hundred years, they found a turnover, not a continuity. The classic Scythians were a new blend, roughly 60% descended from that Bronze Age steppe stock and roughly 40% from an eastern Eurasian, Siberian-related source. The steppe had been remixed. And that eastern fraction was not even across the map. It rose as you moved east and it rose as time went on, so that the Scythians of the Altai carried far more East Asian ancestry than their cousins on the Black Sea, who looked more European. A study of Iron Age nomads by Unterländer and colleagues concluded that the eastern and western Scythians had arisen largely independently, in place, linked by a steady drift of genes from east to west, rather than spreading from a single homeland. “Scythian” really was a culture worn by a genetic mosaic.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aDTn!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F36578c36-57af-468b-9355-8928f9b3210a_1650x2100.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!aDTn!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F36578c36-57af-468b-9355-8928f9b3210a_1650x2100.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Where the western Scythians were read. The 131 genomes Andreeva's team sequenced, drawn from the classic Scythian heartland north of the Black Sea. Andreeva et al., Science Advances 2025.

The western half of that mosaic has now been read too. Andreeva and colleagues sequenced one hundred and thirty-one individuals from the classic Scythian heartland north of the Black Sea. The royal Scythians of the fourth and third centuries BCE turned out to be genetically tight and homogeneous, carrying close to half Yamnaya-derived ancestry, and overwhelmingly of the R1a male line, in some elite clans every single buried man sharing one paternal lineage. Their reconstructed faces, from the pigmentation genes, match Herodotus and the Greek vase painters: mostly brown or fair hair, a fair number with blue eyes. The same study even caught a medical fingerprint of their dominance, a gene variant for fructose intolerance that spread across western Eurasia in the centuries the Scythians ruled the steppe and now sits in the DNA of millions of Europeans.

At a Saka cemetery at Boz-Barmak in Kyrgyzstan, all the men sequenced shared a single Y-lineage, while every individual had a different maternal line, the signature of a society where men stayed put and wives came in from outside. The Pazyryk anthropology had hinted at exactly this decades earlier, when Rudenko found enormous physical variety inside single tombs and guessed at “obligatory exogamy.”

Two caveats before we move on, because this field is still moving fast. The very newest studies, the single Tuva warrior and the Boz-Barmak cemetery, are based on small samples, and one is still a preprint that has not finished peer review. And the headline lineage, R1a-Z93, is reported at coarse resolution in some of the Scythian papers, so treat “the Scythians were Z93” as a strong generalization rather than a claim about every individual.

## V. The peoples they touched: Tocharians, Yuezhi, Greeks, and the road west

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Scythians who ranged from Hungary to Mongolia did not live alone. Over the centuries the Scythians and Sakas traded with, fought, and married into a dozen other peoples, and some of those entanglements get so badly confused in popular accounts (especially on X) that it’s worth taking them one at a time.

The Tocharians first, because the confusion there is nearly total. In the oasis towns of the Tarim Basin, in what is now western China, manuscripts survive in two extinct languages that scholars labeled Tocharian A and Tocharian B. Tocharian is Indo-European, but it is not Iranian, not related to the speech of the Sakas at all. It belongs to a separate and very early branch of the family, and strangely enough it patterns with the western Indo-European tongues like Latin and Celtic, even though it was spoken at the far eastern edge of the Indo-European world. Its ancestors apparently split off near the very beginning and were carried east thousands of years before any Scythian existed, probably with the Afanasievo migration around 3700 BCE.

So the Tocharians and the Sakas were different peoples speaking unrelated languages, separated by millennia. They just ended up as neighbors around the Tarim and Bactria, where they traded and intermarried like neighbors usually do. The famous Tarim Basin mummies make the muddle worse. Those strikingly preserved, sometimes red-haired bodies are forever being paraded as Indo-European, but their genomes say otherwise. A 2021 study found that the earliest Tarim people were a genetically isolated local population, roughly 70% descended from an ancient Ice Age Siberian lineage, with no detectable steppe ancestry whatsoever. They had picked up herding and farming from their neighbors without being replaced by them (yes, this happens, and there are many such examples). So the mummies, the Tocharian language, and the Iranian Sakas are three different things, however stubbornly the popular imagination keeps welding them into one.

The Yuezhi are the ones who matter most here. This was the confederation the Xiongnu drove west, which in turn drove the Sakas into India. But the Yuezhi kept going. One of their five tribes, the Guishuang, rose above the rest and built an empire of its own across Bactria and northern India. These were the Kushans, who would become one of the greatest royal patrons Buddhism ever had. We will come back to them in a bit.

To the southwest, the Sakas met the Greeks of Bactria, the far-flung heirs of Alexander (yawn), who had built a brilliant Hellenistic kingdom on the Oxus and then pushed into India. The Sakas did not destroy this world so much as marry into it. They kept its bilingual coinage and its Greek divine images, kept the court styles, and their kings sometimes carried Greek names. The numismatist Robert Senior showed that you often cannot tell a Saka king from a Greek one by his coins or his name. Further west sat the Parthians, the Iranian dynasty ruling Persia and Mesopotamia, and the Saka and Parthian worlds blurred together in the borderland of Sakastan, or Sistan.

The Scythians’ eastern cousins were eventually absorbed by the Turkic peoples who came to dominate the steppe. Their western relatives had a stranger fate. The Sarmatians, and after them the Alans, rode the grass highway straight into the heart of the late Roman world. Alans fought in Gaul, Iberia, and North Africa, and some scholars trace a contingent of steppe cavalry stationed in Roman Britain (still contested). They carried the culture of the steppe into Europe at the very moment Rome was coming apart. One fragment of them never left the mountains where they took shelter. High in the Caucasus there is a people who still speak an Iranian language descended from that of the Alans, who tell stories reaching back to the Scythians Herodotus described, and who in their own way have kept the old gods. We will meet them at the end. They are the closest thing to living Scythians left on earth.

## VI. How Sakas became Hindus and Buddhists

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Here the story turns. A people whose god was a sword planted in blood, whose kings rode into the earth with strangled retainers, came into India and within a few generations were endowing Buddhist monasteries and turning Greek gods into Hindu ones on their coins. How did that happen?

Partly because the Sakas were never zealots. They were pragmatists ruling over conquered peoples, and Indian religion offered them something their sword cult never could: a way to be legitimate kings of settled subjects.

The Sakas were not the only steppe people to come down the Bactrian road into India. Close behind them came their cousins the Kushans. The Sakas began the turn toward Indian religion; the Kushans, a little later, made it imperial policy.

The clearest place to watch the change is on the coins. Follow one figure across two hundred years of minting, as the scholar Frohlich did, and you can see a god change religions in three stages:

- **Under the Greeks:** he is Poseidon, a purely Greek sea-god holding a
  trident, an odd choice for a landlocked kingdom, but Greek through and
  through.

- **Under the Sakas:** the same trident-bearer is quietly localized. On
  Maues’s coins a small river-god appears at his feet, a detail with no
  Greek parallel; later issues add Iranian flame-markings on his
  shoulders. He is, in Frohlich’s words, “no more Poseidon as the Greeks
  conceived him, but not yet Shiva as the Kushans used to worship him.”

- **Under the Kushans:** he arrives. He stands beside a bull, the
  trident is now a trishula, and the coin names him Oesho. He is
  unmistakably Shiva.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8Ws8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F43543fb6-d822-44cb-a767-e400b1d4d985_1790x1256.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8Ws8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F43543fb6-d822-44cb-a767-e400b1d4d985_1790x1256.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The pantheon on Kushan coins. One dynasty stamped Iranian gods (the fire-god Athsho, the moon Mao, the goddess Nana), Greek ones (Hephaistos, Serapis, Selene), Hindu ones (Oesho, who is Shiva), and the Buddha himself as BODDO, each named in Greek script.

No single king redrew him from scratch. He was reinterpreted coin after coin and reign after reign, a Greek form emptied of its Greek meaning and refilled, first with Iranian content, then with Hindu, until Poseidon’s trident had become Shiva’s trishula.

The Greek strongman Heracles went the same way, sliding toward two Indian fates at once. In Gandhara sculpture he survives as Vajrapani, the Buddha’s guardian, still carrying Heracles’ club, now turned into a thunderbolt. Elsewhere he shades into Balarama, elder brother of Lord Kṛṣṇa.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bBt7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe0940a78-3510-47d5-9640-0a4ff2ab3d1e_600x304.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bBt7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe0940a78-3510-47d5-9640-0a4ff2ab3d1e_600x304.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Shiva facing holding trident and extending right hand.

Rosenfield’s study of their art shows the Kushans kept no state religion at all, a radical thing in their world. Their greatest king, Kanishka, struck gold coins showing himself at a fire altar on one face and, on the other, a rotating cast of gods drawn from every people he ruled, each labeled in Greek letters: the Iranian sun Miiro and moon Mao, the goddess Nana, the fortune-goddess Ardoxsho, the Greek Herakles, the Hindu Oesho who is Shiva, and, most astonishing of all, the Buddha himself, labeled in Greek script BODDO. The earliest datable image of the Buddha on any coin was likely struck by a king descended from steppe nomads. Kanishka went further still. He convened a great Buddhist council, raised a stupa at Peshawar (in present-day Pakistan) that Chinese pilgrims called the tallest building in the world, and funded the missions that carried Buddhism up the Silk Road into China. A faith that began under a tree in Bihar became a world religion in the hands of the children of horse archers.

The royal titles tell the same story of a crown draped in every legitimacy at once. One inscription styles the king:

> *maharajasa rajatirajasa devaputrasa kaisarasa*

Great King, in the Indian style. King of Kings, in the Iranian. Son of God, or Son of Heaven, calqued from the Chinese. And Caesar, from Rome. Four civilizations’ worth of kingship.

And it was not only the emperors. The Western Kshatrapas, the Saka satraps who ruled western India for nearly four centuries, were open-handed Buddhist donors from their first generation. The richest record survives at the Buddhist caves of Nashik, where around 105 CE a Saka nobleman named Ushavadata, son-in-law of the Saka king Nahapana, recorded gift after gift: thousands of gold coins to feed the monks, a cave cut and handed to the order, houses and gardens and water-tanks endowed in town after town along the trade roads, whole herds of cows given away. His wife, Nahapana’s own daughter, endowed a cave of her own. A Saka prince with a Persian name, two generations off the steppe, pouring his fortune into the Buddhist cause.

The precedent for all of it had been set a century earlier, by a Greek. At Besnagar in central India stands a stone pillar raised around 113 BCE by Heliodorus, envoy of the Indo-Greek king Antialcidas, who had become a devotee of Vasudeva, that is, Lord Kṛṣṇa. He called himself a Bhagavata, a follower of the Lord, and crowned his pillar with Viṣṇu’s eagle, Garuda. This pattern outlasted the Sakas. A small engraved seal from Gandhara, now in the British Museum, shows a royal worshipper of steppe stock kneeling before a four-armed Viṣṇu, mace and discus and lotus in his hands, the prayer cut in Bactrian script. Scholars argue over the king, the Kushan Huvishka, or a later Huna or Kidarite prince, but the image is the same either way: a descendant of horse nomads, bowing to Viṣṇu.

## VII. What the Sakas left in India

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Sakas ruled parts of India for the better part of five centuries, longer than the Mughals, and the marks they left are deeper and stranger than most Indians realize, because some of them are things Indians now think of as simply, eternally Indian.

Their rule came in two main waves.

The northern Indo-Scythians, in Gandhara and the Punjab:

- **Maues** (around 120 BCE): the first Saka king in India, who took the
  grand title King of Kings.

- **Azes** (around 58 to 57 BCE): swept away the last Indo-Greek
  kingdoms and unified Gandhara. He even founded a dynastic era of his
  own, the Azes era, long used to date inscriptions across the
  northwest. Some earlier scholars linked the start of the Vikrama
  Samvat era to Azes; this was disproven later.

The western Sakas, the long-lived Western Kshatrapas, who ruled Gujarat, Malwa, and the Konkan from their capital at Ujjain for nearly 380 years:

- **Bhumaka** and **Nahapana**, of the Kshaharata line.

- then **Chastana**, and, greatest of all, **Rudradaman I** (around 150
  CE).

That western Saka kingdom did not go unchallenged. Early in the second century CE the great Deccan power, the Satavahana emperor Gautamiputra Satakarni, fell on Nahapana and destroyed him. The proof survives in metal: a hoard of Nahapana’s silver coins, dug up at Jogalthembi, every one re-struck with Gautamiputra’s own stamp. The long inscription his mother raised at Nashik calls him the destroyer of the Shakas, Yavanas and Pahlavas, the king who uprooted the Kshaharata line and stopped the mixing of the four varnas. For a moment the foreign kings had been driven out.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ztNf!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F62bfc566-0704-4b4b-bafc-9eeccd2d0916_800x392.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ztNf!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F62bfc566-0704-4b4b-bafc-9eeccd2d0916_800x392.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A coin of Nahapana re-struck by Gautamiputra Satakarni

It did not hold. Nahapana’s house was finished, but a second Saka line, the Kardamakas of Chastana, rose in its place, and under Chastana’s grandson Rudradaman the Western Kshatrapas came roaring back, this time beating the Satavahanas in turn. And it is Rudradaman who left India one of its most consequential gifts, though it is the last gift you would expect from a man the orthodox texts called a fallen barbarian.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!v68K!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a1ed4cd-ea43-4390-8705-0dd411a853e3_1280x960.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!v68K!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a1ed4cd-ea43-4390-8705-0dd411a853e3_1280x960.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Junagadh rock inscription

On a great rock at Junagadh in Gujarat, the same rock that already bore an edict of the emperor Ashoka, Rudradaman had carved a long inscription recording how he repaired a broken irrigation dam, the Sudarshana lake, at his own expense, without burdening his subjects with new taxes or forced labor. The dam is not the point. The language is. Every major Indian royal inscription before Rudradaman, the edicts of Ashoka, the records of the Satavahanas, the dedications of the Indo-Greeks, had been written in Prakrit, the everyday vernacular. Rudradaman’s Junagadh inscription is the first long royal inscription in Sanskrit.

And then there is the calendar that did not stop. The Shaka era, beginning in 78 CE, now most often linked to the accession of the Saka ruler Chastana. The Shaka era spread across India and Southeast Asia, was used to date temples and dynasties for nearly two millennia, and in 1957 the Republic of India adopted it, as the Shaka calendar, for its official national reckoning. Open a government of India gazette and the date is given in the era of the Sakas. A people who came to India as refugees off the steppe gave the modern Indian state the very name of its year.

The Sakas also left art. Under their patronage and that of the Kushans, the Gandhara school fused Greek technique with Indian devotion to produce, for the first time, the Buddha in human form, the serene face that would be copied from Afghanistan to Japan. They left an administrative model, the satrap-as-viceroy system, that later dynasties borrowed. They left their coinage standard, so prized that when the Gupta emperor Chandragupta II finally conquered the last Western Kshatrapa around 400 CE, he kept minting coins in the loser’s design.

## VIII. How the Mahabharata judged them

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Indian literature did not ignore the Sakas. It wrestled with them, and the way it did so reveals something subtle about how Indian civilization handled outsiders, very differently from how the Greeks and other Indo-Europeans handled theirs.

In the Mahabharata, the Sakas (Shakas) appear again and again, almost always in the same company: the Yavanas (Greeks), the Kambojas, the Pahlavas (Parthians), the Tusharas, the Pulindas, the Kiratas. They are foreign warrior peoples on the edges of the known world. They turn up in the great catalogues of the Kaurava army, fighting in their thousands. They appear at Yudhishthira’s imperial sacrifice, bringing tribute of horses “that could travel great and long distances.” They are, in the epic’s geography, real and powerful peoples who must be reckoned with.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!H39Q!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5743fca-fbaa-43cf-926e-10ef83cde954_1536x1024.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!H39Q!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5743fca-fbaa-43cf-926e-10ef83cde954_1536x1024.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**At Yudhishthira’s Rājasūya sacrifice in the** ***Sabha Parva*****, the Mahābhārata gives a long catalogue of peoples who bring tribute to the Pāṇḍava king.** The Śakas/Scythians appear in this list alongside other frontier and regional peoples.

But the epic also explains what is wrong with them. The key passage comes in the Shanti Parva:

> It is because there are no brahmanas among them that Shakas, Yavanas,
> Kambojas and other similar kshatriya tribes are regarded as the equals
> of vrishalas. It is because there are no brahmanas among them that
> Dramilas, Kalingas, Pulindas, Ushinaras, Kolas, Sarpas, Mahishakas and
> other similar kshatriya tribes are regarded as the equals of
> vrishalas.

The Shakas are called kshatriya tribes. Their fallen status, equal to a vrishala or shudra, is explicitly conditional: it holds “because there are no brahmanas among them,” because they have lost the priestly scaffolding of ritual, not because of anything innate in their blood. And, most tellingly of all, the Shakas and Yavanas are named in the very same breath as the Dramilas, the Tamils, and the Kalingas and Pulindas, peoples of India itself. This is not a wall built to keep foreigners out.

The epic even imagines these peoples being born inside the Brahmanical cosmos. In the story of the sage Vasishtha and his miraculous cow, when the cow is attacked she generates whole armies from her own body to defend her master, and among the warriors she produces are the Pahlavas, the Shakas, and the Yavanas, brought forth from her dung and her urine and her foam. It is hardly flattering, but notice what it does: it places the steppe and Mediterranean peoples inside the Indian sacred order, as warriors summoned to defend a brahmana.

There is a more famous degradation story, the one where King Sagara, advised by his guru, spares the Shakas, Yavanas, Kambojas, Paradas, and Pahlavas but punishes them by shaving their heads and stripping them of the Vedas. It is worth a careful note, because it is often misattributed: that tale lives in the Harivamsha and the Puranas, not in the main body of the Mahabharata.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4LsZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2aab67ee-f72c-4b7d-9ca0-a83cda53b3d1_593x1230.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4LsZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2aab67ee-f72c-4b7d-9ca0-a83cda53b3d1_593x1230.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Scythian soldier, Nagarjunakonda,Andhra Pradesh.

Set this against how the Greeks saw the Scythians. To the Greek imagination, as the scholar Edith Hall has shown, the Scythian was the archetypal barbarian, the savage opposite against whom Greekness defined itself, milk-drinking, lawless, alien by essence. The Indian view was harsher in its language, but softer in its logic. The Shaka was a kshatriya who had lost his way and could, in principle, be brought back into the fold. And that, in the end, is precisely what happened: the Sakas married into Indian dynasties, endowed Indian temples and monasteries, wrote inscriptions in Sanskrit, and dissolved into the Indian population. As the historian Chattopadhyaya put it in a line that sums up the whole drama:

> Ancient India has always captured her captors. The Sakas were also
> captured and completely Indianised.

## IX. Where the children of the Scythians are now

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

So where did they all go? The answer comes in three parts: one solid, one contested, and one that is a calendar.

**1. The solid part: the Ossetians.** High in the Caucasus live the Ossetians, a people of around two and a half million who still speak an Iranian language. They descend from the Alans, who descended from the Sarmatians, who were the eastern cousins and successors of the Scythians. They are the closest thing to living Scythians on earth, and the proof is not only in their genes but in their stories. Their great oral epic, the Nart sagas, preserves across some twenty-five centuries the recognizable bones of what Herodotus reported. Georges Dumézil spent decades matching the two. Among the survivals:

- divination by bundles of rods, just as Herodotus describes the
  Scythian diviners

- the sacred blood-brotherhood oath, sworn over a shared cup of wine and
  blood

- the bow as the symbol of kingship

- a magical self-filling cup that rises to the lips of whoever tells the
  truth

- a forty-day mourning, and taboos that echo the Iranian Avesta

A reviewer of Dumézil called the Nart sagas “a treasure of epic narratives that confirms, illuminates, enriches the meagre ancient materials” we have from antiquity.

And the Narts give us the most beautiful ending this story could ask for. Their greatest hero is Batradz, a warrior with a body of tempered steel, forged and hardened in the sea by the smith of the gods. Batradz cannot die while his great sword remains in the world. At the end, dying, he commands his comrades to throw his sword into the sea. Twice they lie and say they have done it; twice he knows from the calm of the waters that they have not. Only when the sword finally goes in, and the sea turns to blood and storm, does Batradz die. If that rings a bell, it should. It is the death of King Arthur, Excalibur returned to the lake, a thousand years and three thousand miles from the Caucasus. And it is, scholars argue, the same image Herodotus saw on the steppe: the sword that is a god, the blade that holds a hero’s life. The Scythian Ares, the iron sword in the brushwood mound, rode west into the Caucasus as Batradz’s blade, and rode further west still into the legend of Camelot. The most famous sword in the European imagination may be a Scythian one.

**2. The contested part: India.** This is where it gets sensitive, so it is worth stating precisely what the evidence supports. Several martial communities of the northwest carry long traditions, many of them hardened in the colonial period, of Scythian or Saka descent:

- the Jats

- some Rajput clans

- the Ahirs

- the Gujjars

- and, even more loosely, the Pashtuns

The romance is understandable, since these are warrior communities in exactly the regions the Sakas ruled. But the genetics does not support a clean “we are the Scythians” story for any of them. The most careful study of Jat Y-chromosomes found at least nine distinct ancestral sources, with no single origin, Scythian or otherwise, accounting for them. The careful way to put it: some northwestern lineages may well carry a Saka thread, real but partial, woven into a population that is, like every Indian population, a deep and ancient mixture.

But the Sakas have far more Indian descendants than any caste genealogy could capture, and it has nothing to do with blood. They were absorbed. That is the whole point, the thing that makes the Indian story the opposite of the Scythian story in Europe: the Sakas did not stay a separate people who could be tracked. They married in, worshipped in, gave in, ruled in, and dissolved. Their genes scattered through the northwest in fragments too mixed to label.

**3. The part that needs no DNA: the calendar.** Every time the government of India prints an official date in the Shaka era, the era that still bears the Sakas' name, the Scythians are still here. They have no monuments that crowds visit, no nation that bears their name, no language anyone in India still speaks. But they left their fingerprint on the most ordinary thing of all, the count of the years, and a billion people carry it without knowing whose it is.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WpJ1!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F23832c8a-8e1f-41d0-84a2-1a2ac6f39f36_1416x1111.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WpJ1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F23832c8a-8e1f-41d0-84a2-1a2ac6f39f36_1416x1111.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Eugène Delacroix,** ***Ovid among the Scythians*****, 1859.** The painting imagines the Roman poet Ovid during his exile on the Black Sea frontier, living among the Scythians after being banished from Rome by Augustus.

## Sources and further reading

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Classical sources**

- Herodotus, *Histories*, Book IV (the Scythian account: origins, the
  seven gods, the sword of Ares IV.62, the Enarei, royal burials
  IV.71-72, the hemp purification IV.73-75) and Book I (Cyrus and
  Tomyris of the Massagetae, I.201-214).

- Hippocrates, *Airs, Waters, Places* (on the Enarei).

**Scythian history, religion, and art**

- Ellis H. Minns, *Scythians and Greeks* (Cambridge, 1913), the
  foundational survey; source of the pantheon and burial translations
  quoted here.

- Sergei I. Rudenko, *Frozen Tombs of Siberia: The Pazyryk Burials of
  Iron Age Horsemen* (trans. 1970), the Pazyryk excavations: tattoos,
  embalming, the hemp apparatus, the oldest pile carpet, the felt
  goddess.

- M. I. Artamonov, *Treasures from Scythian Tombs* (1969), the Hermitage
  gold and Greco-Scythian art.

- S. S. Bessonova, on the reconstruction of the Scythian pantheon
  (Iranian, hearth-centered).

- Edith Hall, *Inventing the Barbarian* (Oxford, 1989), the Greek
  construction of the Scythian as savage other.

**Genetics**

- Allentoft et al. (2015, *Nature*): Bronze Age Eurasia, Andronovo, R1a.

- Unterländer et al. (2017, *Nature Communications*): Iron Age nomads,
  east-west independence and asymmetry.

- Damgaard et al. (2018, *Nature*): 137 steppe genomes, Saka admixture.

- Krzewińska et al. (2018, *Science Advances*): Cimmerians, Scythians,
  Sarmatians; the Srubnaya source.

- Gnecchi-Ruscone et al. (2021, *Science Advances*): 111 genomes, the
  Bronze-to-Iron-Age turnover (about 60 percent steppe, 40 percent
  eastern Eurasian).

- Andreeva et al. (2025, *Science Advances*): 131 western Scythians, R1a
  dominance, phenotype.

- Rymbekova et al. (2025, preprint): Saka of Boz-Barmak, female exogamy
  (unreviewed, treat as provisional).

- Nedoluzhko et al. (2025, *BMC Genomics*): the Chinge-Tey warrior and
  the eastern-origin case.

- Zhang et al. (2021, *Nature*): the Tarim Basin mummies as a local
  Ancient North Eurasian population, not steppe migrants.

- Mahal & Matsoukas (2017, *Frontiers in Genetics*): Jat Y-chromosomes,
  nine ancestral origins, against any single-origin Scythian claim.

**Sakas in India**

- B. N. Shrava, *The Sakas in India* (1947); D. C. Sircar and S.
  Chattopadhyaya, *The Sakas in India* (1955): the migration,
  Indianization, and the “captured her captors” thesis.

- Robert Senior, *Indo-Scythian Coins and History*: Maues, Azes, the
  single-Azes revision, the Vikrama era.

- John Rosenfield, *The Dynastic Arts of the Kushans* (1967): Kanishka,
  the coin pantheon, religious pluralism.

- Frohlich, on the deities of Indo-Scythian coins (Poseidon to Shiva,
  Heracles to Vajrapani and Balarama).

- The Junagadh rock inscription of Rudradaman I (around 150 CE), ed.
  Kielhorn, *Epigraphia Indica* VIII; the Nashik cave inscriptions of
  Ushavadata; the Heliodorus pillar at Besnagar.

- The Mahabharata (Bibek Debroy translation): the Shanti Parva passage
  on the Shakas as fallen kshatriyas; the Vasishtha cow myth; the army
  and tribute catalogues.

**Descendants and survivals**

- Georges Dumézil, work on the Ossetian Nart sagas and Scythian
  continuity; V. I. Abaev on the Narts; on Batradz and the
  sword-in-the-sea, see the *Encyclopaedia Iranica* entry “Batradz.”

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1
